content:
{"novelty":4,"feasibility":8,"effectiveness":3,"overall":4}

This idea has significant overlap with existing self-reflection and self-correction prompting methods (e.g., "Reflexion," "Self-Refine," "Self-Verification"). The core mechanism—having the model critique its own solution and revise—has been explored extensively in recent literature. The "counterfactual" framing adds minimal conceptual novelty beyond asking the model to consider alternative paths, which is already implicit in reflection-based approaches.

Feasibility is high: the method requires only API calls with no training, and the experimental setup (GSM8K, MATH benchmarks) is standard and accessible. A student could implement and test this within 1-2 months.

Effectiveness is questionable. Empirical evidence suggests LLMs struggle to reliably identify their own errors without external feedback—self-correction often fails or even degrades performance on math tasks when the model lacks ground truth. The method assumes the model can accurately enumerate its failure modes, which is precisely what it struggles with. Strong baselines like self-consistency with majority voting are difficult to beat without fundamentally new information.

Overall, this represents an incremental variation on well-explored self-correction themes without sufficient novelty or a compelling reason to expect it will overcome known limitations of such approaches. It would likely be rejected at major venues for lack of novelty and insufficient differentiation from prior work.
